name: Slide Hampton
abstract: Locksley Wellington Hampton (April 21, 1932 – November 18, 2021) was an American jazz trombonist, composer and arranger. As his nickname implies, Hampton's main instrument was slide trombone, but he also occasionally played tuba and flugelhorn.

interviews.title: Slide Hampton interviewed by Monk Rowe, West Orange, New Jersey, July 30, 2003

quotes.text: Besides practicing, there was a contest that Lionel Hampton’s band was set up to do this. They were set up to do this, and part of the attraction to wanting to win this competition was that you were going to get to play – open the show for Lionel Hampton’s band. We got votes and got people to vote for us. We got enough votes to win this contest. We won it, and we played Carnegie Hall, opening the show for Lionel Hampton’s band.
quotes.caption: Winning a contest to open for Lionel Hampton at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: I don’t remember. What I do remember is, though, that they had – at that time they had a conga drummer in the band. They had two drummers. It was the most moving thing that I had ever witnessed. They had the people in Carnegie Hall almost jumping out of the balconies. It was really wonderful for me, because it was New York. It was a chance to hear music played on this level. Because, see, a lot of people don’t know about Lionel Hampton’s bands. He had some fantastic bands.
quotes.caption: Slide Hampton recalls Lionel Hampton's band at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: Trombonist and arranger Slide Hampton was born on April 21, 1932 in Jeannette, Pennsylvania. He has performed with Woody Herman, Maynard Ferguson and Dizzy Gillespie, and played a major role in Gillespie’s United Nations Orchestra. His arrangements have been performed by numerous ensembles including the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band and the Collective Black Artists’ Orchestra.
quotes.caption: Biographical summary of Slide Hampton's career
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton interviewed by Monk Rowe, West Orange, New Jersey, July 30, 2003

quotes.text: And in jazz, first it was the music and then there was the dance you see. So that was a very inspiring part of it but we also finally played at Carnegie Hall, Apollo Theater, and again the Savoy Ballroom, where everybody is dancing again, like a thousand people are dancing, the floor is going up and down like this. And actually we played the Apollo, and then we went in the Savoy for two weeks, and we came, by popular demand, back in the Apollo again. And nobody ever did that. And the thing was that see in the Apollo you have these big shows. You have a lot of music. We didn’t read music. We had a couple of brothers that read but the rest of us didn’t read. They had to teach us the part. So we would learn the parts by memory and we played the show. And the people, they loved it.
quotes.caption: Slide Hampton mentions playing Carnegie Hall among other venues
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton interviewed by Monk Rowe, West Orange, New Jersey, July 30, 2003

quotes.text: The Carnegie Hall Band, yeah. I didn’t do a very good job.
quotes.caption: Slide Hampton recalls poor performance with Carnegie Hall Band
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton interviewed by Monk Rowe, West Orange, New Jersey, July 30, 2003

quotes.text: It’s very difficult to find a drummer. It’s very difficult. There are a lot of drummers out there, and they’re good drummers, but still – very difficult to find one that will have the sensitivity, have the understanding of orchestration, because a drummer – I went to see a concert at Carnegie Hall. Carnegie Hall is very alive, sound-wise. This guy would hit a crash that would cover up four bars of music. He’s a very good drummer, but the only thing is, he don’t understand about orchestration. When you have the orchestra playing some harmony that is beautiful and important, you can’t hit a crash, because the crash has overtones in it that will cancel a lot of that music out. That’s like playing a B- flat diminished chord in the right hand and a B diminished chord. Cancels out all the music. There’s no harmony going to come out of that. You probably can do the same thing with an augmented chord. A B-flat augmented and a B augmented probably will cancel out all the music. A cymbal can crash and just cover up bars of music. The drummer has to know that, because otherwise, half the concert, the people will never hear it. They never hear – the trumpet’s playing in a mute, and the cat’s hitting bam on the cymbal.
quotes.caption: Hampton recalls a drummer's poor orchestration sensitivity
quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie McLean
about_quotes.evidence: Slide Hampton, Jon Faddis, James Moody, myself on the front line.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie McLean — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Holly Hofmann
about_quotes.evidence: That came about through another wonderful jazz musician named Slide Hampton, who heard me at a Jamey Aebersold clinic when I was in college, and we struck up a friendship and a long — years later we still get a chance to play together upon occasion.
about_quotes.doc_title: Holly Hofmann interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 13, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Lanny Morgan
about_quotes.evidence: Slide Hampton was on the band quite a bit off and on... And so many guys went through that band. You know the saxes when I joined were Frank Hittner was playing baritone and Joe Farrell was playing tenor and Willie and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lanny Morgan interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: By this time we were also going over to the Hampton's house to practice. They had a family band... That was me and David Young and the guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: the first gig I got was with Slide Hampton and his octet... So I worked with Slide and that group for a while.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: within about two weeks I did a job with Slide Hampton. We went down to North Carolina and played a concert.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001
